policy_statement: Indonesia sets an adaptation pathways goal to reduce climate change impacts on national GDP loss by 3.45% in 2050 by increasing resilience in food, water, energy, and environmental health across economic, social/livelihood, and ecosystem/landscape resilience areas.
mechanism_description: Sets an adaptation pathways goal to increase resilience in food, water, energy, and environmental health, strengthening adaptive capacity to reduce climate-change impacts on the economy.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard → improved planning/response capacity → ↓ exposure & ↓ vulnerability → ↓ climate impacts/losses
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Government establishes an adaptation pathways goal and associated cross-sector resilience objectives, strengthening institutions, governance, and decision-making processes for climate risk management (e.g., coordinated planning across food, water, energy, health/environment).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Improved anticipatory planning, preparedness, and adaptive management reduce the sensitivity of systems to climate stressors (droughts, floods, heat, storms, ecosystem degradation) and improve the ability to maintain services under stress.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced adverse impacts from climate change—lower disruption to essential services and reduced economic losses (e.g., avoided GDP loss), i.e., increased resilience rather than emissions reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing climate hazard impacts (economic and societal damages) by increasing adaptive capacity and resilience across critical systems (food, water, energy, environment/health).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism primarily reduces climate impacts by improving adaptive capacity (planning, governance, preparedness) across sectors—i.e., hazard → reduced vulnerability/impact.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While better management can sometimes yield co-benefits (efficiency improvements or mitigation), the stated intent and main effect are to reduce climate-change damages (e.g., GDP loss) through resilience and adaptive capacity, which is squarely Adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an “adaptation pathways” goal explicitly includes emissions targets or energy-efficiency mandates as the main lever, mitigation/resource-efficiency could become dominant; however, for this canonical mechanism (goal → adaptive capacity), the core effect is impact reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that uses this mechanism—setting adaptation pathways goals to build institutional adaptive capacity—has its largest climate-relevant effect in reducing vulnerability and losses from climate hazards, regardless of sector emphasis (food/water/energy/health).  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer labeling (e.g., drought resilience, health system resilience, economic resilience, governance capacity) depends on policy-specific details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Edge case: Some “pathways” frameworks can bundle mitigation and adaptation; if the dominant implemented actions are decarbonization measures rather than risk reduction, the primary category could shift. For the stated mechanism (institutional adaptive capacity to reduce impacts/economic loss), Adaptation remains dominant.
key_indicators: “sets the goal of adaptation pathways”; “reduce the impact of climate change on national GDP loss by 3.45% in 2050”; “increasing resilience”; “four basic necessities (food, water, energy, and environmental health)”; “three target areas of resilience”
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a broader planning-strategy framework (adaptation pathways), but the presence of a quantified outcome goal (GDP loss reduction by 3.45% by 2050) makes target-setting the best fit.
